seoTitle: All the Ways to Share a Microsoft Forms Survey
metaDescription: Compare Microsoft Forms sharing options — public links, org-only access, QR codes, embed tags, and collaboration links — and pick the right one.
intro: Distributing a survey the wrong way can either expose it to the wrong audience or lock out the people you need to reach. Microsoft Forms gives you several distribution formats — links, QR codes, embeds, and collaboration invites — and this guide breaks down when to use each.

steps.heading: Open the sharing drawer
steps.body: Click the Collect Responses button in the upper-right corner of your form editor to open the sharing options panel.

steps.heading: Set your access rule
steps.body: Use the audience dropdown to choose between Anyone can respond or Only people in my org, depending on how public the survey should be.

steps.heading: Shorten the link
steps.body: Check the Shorten URL box to generate a clean, compressed link before copying it to distribute.

steps.heading: Grab a QR code or embed tag
steps.body: Toggle the shortcut icons to switch from a raw link to a downloadable QR code or a website embed tag.

steps.heading: Invite collaborators separately
steps.body: Scroll to the Collaborate section and generate a co-authoring link so teammates can view and edit results without submitting a response themselves.

faq.question: What's the difference between a sharing link and a collaboration link?
faq.answer: A sharing link is for respondents to submit answers, while a collaboration link invites teammates to view or edit the form itself.

faq.question: Can I embed a form directly into my website?
faq.answer: Yes, the sharing drawer includes an iframe embed tag you can paste directly into a web page.

faq.question: Should I restrict a form to my organization only?
faq.answer: Restricting access to your organization is safer for internal data collection, while public access suits open surveys or market research.

faq.question: Does the QR code update automatically if I shorten the link?
faq.answer: Yes, shortening the URL also updates the density and appearance of the generated QR code.
